videos.title: URUGUAY is SCIENTIFICALLY BETTER than your Country (and mine) 🇺🇾
videos.description:
URUGUAY is the happiest country in South America. And I had to know why. Why Uruguay? I went to Montevideo & Punta del Este Beach in order to figure out what Uruguay is doing so well... and I found out that there is actually quite a lot that we can all stand to learn from them!

***

Looking for Some THINGS TO DO in Montevideo?

Punta Del Este Day Trip: vi.me/fBigo
Colonia Day Trip: vi.me/YwfXv
Half Day Montevideo City Tour: vi.me/xVSVO

These are affiliate Links! If you're planning to book, it helps my channel if you use these links and it would mean a lot! ❤️

***

Videos to Watch Next:

Guatemala CHANGED my LIFE: https://youtu.be/hB1gU5-tYos

How I FAILED in Peru: https://youtu.be/XviEULhCG1k

INSANE Story About El Salvador!: https://youtu.be/sjI8m6sLzOo

***

Follow me on Instagram: www.instagram.com/j.m.payne
Follow me on Tiktok: www.tiktok.com/@josh.payne.travels

***

My name is Josh, and this channel is my chance to connect with fellow travelers. I've been to 45 countries and my dream is to set foot on every nation on the planet. I hope that my channel can bring you some joy, entertainment, and laughter as we build our travel & food itineraries together.

I'm always looking to connect with like-minded individuals! I hope you'll send me your favorite travel channels so we can build a community together right here on Youtube. I'd love to hear what you're loving, what you'd like to see me do next, or where you're itching for more travel information from, in every city and every nation on planet Earth.


00:00 INTRO
00:57 HAPPIEST COUNTRY
03:41 CREATION & RECREATION
05:14 ARTISTRY OF URUGUAY
07:09 SHOULD YOU VISIT?
videos.transcript: Okay lean in, settle down, come in close and grab yourself a hot mug of mate, because you and i need to have a discussion about the nation of uruguay. Yes, you heard that right, uruguay. If you're not from south america, you would be forgiven if you were unable to even pinpoint uruguay on a map. So i'm going to help you out: it is this little nation right here, the one tucked in between argentina and brazil, and for several reasons which i plan to speak about in this video, uruguay has secretly assembled one of the most successful, prosperous, and happiest nations in the entire world. And if you couldn't tell, i absolutely loved spending a week there. My name is josh, and i'm a digital nomad who lives in a different country every single month. Join me as we dive into the magic and the mysteries of uruguay by visiting two of its most famous cities: the wealthy beach town of punta del este, and the capital city of montevideo. I'm not really sure how it's possible to scientifically measure happiness. Uh and and yet somehow people do it every single year. New reports come out detailing which countries around the world have the happiest populations, and we have to start there because in south america it is uruguay. Yeah, uruguay. uh not brazil with its carnival and its samba dance parties, not argentina with its tango and its gorgeous patagonian terrain. Uh nope, in most years it is uruguay that is named the happiest country in south america. In fact, in a survey from 2022, nearly half of all uruguayan people regardless of their age, or gender, or sexual orientation, described themselves as being "very happy". Nearly half! And as crazy as that number seems, it really didn't take me very long after landing into montevideo to figure out why. Why? First and foremost money there is money in uruguay. Okay? Whoever said money doesn't bring you happiness was apparently lying. In addition to being the happiest nation in south america, it is also the most expensive country on the continent to live or to visit. And unlike over in the U.S.A. where the money mostly lives in the hands of a few billionaires, the thing about uruguay is that very broadly speaking, the money is much more spread around compared to every other nation in south america. Uruguay has quite a large middle class, and comparatively low levels of poverty and extreme poverty. As a result, there is much less crime. It's actually a a very safe nation overall, and you'll feel that if you choose to visit. You will feel very comfortable walking around montevideo despite the fact that it's a major capital city. The country is also, generally speaking, very open, very progressive. It was one of the first nations in the region to allow same-sex marriage, and was in fact the very first nation on the entire planet to legalize the recreational use of cannabis for locals back in 2013. And, perhaps most impressively, it's its climate policies that are making waves around the globe. At this moment around 98%, yeah 98% of uruguay's power nationwide, comes through renewable resources. It's proof that even a nation that is growing and becoming more and more economically stable does not have to sacrifice forward-thinking policies. Add all of this stuff together and you get a whole bunch of happy people. And when people are feeling good they have more time to enjoy themselves and to create. Beyond the financial situation, the people of uruguay seem to have a strong appreciation for instituting work life balance. It's sort of ingrained into the culture. In fact, in the old town center of montevideo, one of the most popular tourist areas of the city, the high majority of sites close very early. My hotel was in this area, and i had to make sure that i got dinner on time because by 8 or 9:00, which is the time that dinner service honestly begins in most of south america, most places in oldtown were already closed. Most notably, the very famous food center Mercado del puerto closes every day as early as 6:00 p.m. the food inside, however, is really amazing. In fact, be sure to subscribe because my next video is all about the food in montevideo, including one of the best meals i ever had right here in this building. But the thing is that you have to go for lunch. Now there are other reasons for this, the old town is the business and banking district. However, as a tourist it's a little frustrating. "We want people available and serving us at every hour of the day, damn it!" All selfishness aside, you can, you know, you can see though why this country is happy. It just makes sense when people aren't so stressed about money all the time, they in turn choose to use their time toward their passions, which for us humans, usually means either making art or enjoying recreational activities. The artistry and recreation of uruguay just blew me away. It was probably the biggest takeaway from my visit. Now you'll see examples of this everywhere you go in montevideo, but my favorites are actually closer to punte del este beach over in the east, which is where i had the opportunity to visit casapueblo. This incredibly designed space is currently a museum, but it was formerly the home and the workspace of its architect an artist named Carlos Páez Vilaró. It is stunning and it is just one example of the incredible appreciation for art found in uruguay. Nearby you can see the even more famous hand sculpture known simply as "la mano". It's at punta del este beach, and it was created by the chilean artist Mario Irarrázabal. And of course back in the capital city montevideo is just littered with museums. I went to four different museums during my week there, including Museo Joaquin torres garcia, which highlights the work of the man responsible for that super famous sketch of the upside down version of south america. Now of course, not everyone wants to make art, other people choose to spend their free time with sports or relaxing at the beach. And many locals in uruguay do just that, they spend their time in the evenings enjoying themselves and relaxing, and there is tons of that in montevideo. Pocito beach, the most famous beach in town, is easily accessible from many parts of the city. Even on an ordinary weekday it was just loaded with people, mostly locals playing sports, and getting a tan, and overall just having a lovely evening. With all that, yeah you can absolutely see why the people are happy. It starts with a better financial system, and combined with better work life balance people just generally lead happier lives. And truly it was a beautiful thing to witness first-hand. Very few visitors come to uruguay from north america and europe, and i'm not totally sure how the people of uruguay feel about this. Do they want more tourism, or are they content to continue to be the best kept secret of south america? In my experience, from the uruguayans that i had the chance to speak with, they are very eager and excited to share their culture with the outside world. For a long time they've sort of been undermined in tourism by their neighbors brazil and argentina, but you should know that they absolutely have their own culture, and their own way of life. There are similarities, sure, uh especially with argentina, but if you actually visit for yourself you'll have the chance to sort of dive into the nuance of it all. The different ways of speaking, the the different energy from the people, and without a doubt there is that happiness. And all of that joy sort of rubs off on you as the visitor. So yes go, go to uruguay. Look i'll even make the planning easier for you, take a trip to buenos aires, sure, but then just take the ferry over it's super easy spend a couple days in colonia, and montevideo, and and just feel it out for yourself. If i'm being honest, uruguay seems to have it all figured out, and we should all be taking lessons from the way that they are running their country. I love uruguay, but i also love you! Wow, thank you so much for watching. If you've made it this far, drop a comment to say hi, i always try to respond to the nice ones. And please don't forget to like and subscribe. This is another video that youtube thinks you are really going to like, I hope you give it a try!

safetyNotes: As a result, there is much less crime
It's actually a a very safe nation overall, and you'll feel that if you choose to visit
And please don't forget to like and subscribe

living in uruguay pros and cons
videos.transcript: So, if you're looking to retire in Monte Vido, Uruguay, this video is for you. In this video, I'm going to go over the pros and cons of retiring in Monte Vido, Uruguay. First of all, where is Monte Vido Uruguay? Well, it is a neighboring country to Brazil and Argentina. It's about a 2 and 1/2 hour boat ride back there from Monte Vido to Buenes Aries, Argentina. So, first I'm going to go over the pros. It's a very high quality of life here. Literally walking around for 5 days, you can definitely feel the quality of life here. Urguay is often considered one of the safest places in all of Latin America. It's also very, very clean. That was one thing that we noticed right off the bat after driving into Monte Vido from the airport. It was like being back home in Canada or the US. Next, the affordable cost of living. I did another video on the cost of living in Monte Vido right up here, but for the sake of this video, the cost of living generally speaking is a lot lower than what you're going to pay in Canada or the United States of America. Next, if you're retiring anywhere, you want to look at health care. And the health care system here is awesome. Urguay boasts a very well- reggarded health care system and Medicare facilities. Now, in Monte Vido, the climate is quite mild. It's warm in the summer and mild in the winter. Right now, we are in March 2025. It's quite windy, as you can probably hear on the microphone. Uh, it's relatively cold in the mornings and warmer in the afternoons. It's not like Central America or the tropics, but it is very, very doable without a hoodie or a heavy jacket. And we heard last week was about 40° C, which is about high 90s or even in the hundreds. Uruguay boasts some of the best beaches. So, you've got Puna Dstee, beautiful beaches. It's kind of like old Miami in Monte Vido. They've got quite a few beaches from these drone shots. As you can see, yellowishbased sand and although the water can be quite cold because it it is river/ ocean water. Another big plus is the rich culture. When you come here, you definitely notice how much culture and how people integrate here. It's really a great site. You definitely feel like you're in Italy or Spain, not really in South America anymore. And another huge perk if you're retiring here is the tax benefits for retirees. For more on the tax benefits in Monte Vido, I did a video with Carlos from Angel Vulker's Monte Vido where he talks about this right here. Okay, so now I'm going to get into the cons. Biggest con I would say is possibly the language barrier. Although a lot of places they do understand English, they might not speak English back to you very well. They do sometimes speak it. It's not as much as you would get in Mexico or in Panama, but it is prominent. So that is why it's a con because not everybody does speak English. A con is it's a much slower pace of life. We noticed walking around it was almost like an eerie feeling. Nobody is around. You're you're walking around and you're like, "Well, where is everybody?" Uh, at nighttime, literally past like 9:00, you're it's like everything is closed except for the restaurants. It's kind of a weird feeling because it's a much slower pace of life. Next, limited job opportunities. There's just not a lot of job opportunities if you're a foreigner coming here to retire. Now, if you're looking to import, export, or get things that you would get back in the United States or Canada, things can really, really vary in price because we are in South America. And last but not least, public transportation. So, now the buses, they are prominent. You do see them. They run for about a dollar each way, but they don't always come. And sometimes when it's windy like this, it is kind of cold, so you could be sitting there for a while. They do have Ubers and they do have taxis. Ubers are relatively cheap, but that can add up if you're doing an Uber here and Uber back and Uber here and Uber back. It can get expensive. So, if you like this video, make sure to give it a like, subscribe to the channel, and let me know what your biggest takeaway was for Monte Vido Uruguay. And please watch these other videos about Monte Video.

safetyNotes: They run for about a dollar each way, but they don't always come

videos.title: Things To Do in MONTEVIDEO | Is the Capital of Uruguay Worth Visiting?!

In this video, we discuss the fact that it is more expensive to visit Uruguay than most other South American countries, but also offer some useful tips on how to get the most out of your budget! We visit some historical places in the city and give our opinion on whether or not a trip to Mondevideo is really worth it!

videos.title: Cost of Living in Montevideo, Uruguay and Visiting Colonia del Sacramento too
videos.description:
We've heard from many travelers that Uruguay is one of the most expensive countries they've visited in Latin America. So we're going to check out the prices of goods and services, including food, rent, and transportation, in Uruguay's capital, Montevideo. And then we'll also compare them to the costs in the smaller touristy town of Colonia del Sacramento, about 2 and a half hours away.

#earlyretirement #slowtravel #uruguay
videos.transcript: We're in the waterfront city of Montevideo, Uruguay. It's a beautiful place and I just love hanging out by the shore watching people fish and drink yerba mate. But we also heard that Uruguay can be really expensive. So let's see what things cost here. We've heard from so many travelers that Uruguay is one of the most expensive places they visited in all of Latin America. So we're going to check out the prices of goods and services here in Uruguay's capital city of Montevideo. And then we'll also compare them to the costs in the smaller touristy town of Colonia de Sacramento about 2.5 hours away. We're going to give all of our prices in US dollars but we pay for everything here with either the local currency of the Uruguayan peso or with credit cards. First let's talk about getting around the city using buses, taxis, and ride shares. We couldn't seem to get an Uber here. We tried twice but the app just kept looking for drivers without ever allocating us one. So we gave up and started taking taxis instead. A 15-minute taxi ride cost about $6 or $7. We paid for our taxi rides with the Uruguayan peso but the drivers told us that they also accept credit cards. Buses cost us 55 pesos per ride and so we just gave the driver cash and they gave us change. The buses were really easy to figure out using Google Maps. I did find the signs with street names on them to be quite interesting because they had advertising on them. I'm not sure I've ever seen anything like that before. Unlike most of the countries we visit we didn't get a physical SIM when we arrived here. That's because as we were on the ferry into Uruguay we received a text from Claro, our service provider in Argentina, that we could buy a roaming package in Uruguay for about $7.50 so we just did that instead. Now let's talk about restaurants. This is probably where we really started to notice why people say that Uruguay is expensive. When we first arrived we went out for what we thought was a simple lunch and the bill ended up coming to $40. We did notice that the restaurants in Montevideo seem to give a discount if you pay by credit card though. Our restaurant bill was originally about $45 when we first got the bill but then because we paid by credit card it came out to about $40. The restaurants here seem really interesting to me though. I really love the way the ones in the Mercado del Puerto were all set up. They looked really inviting to me. Grocery stores also seemed really expensive to us, especially after coming from a month in Buenos Aires. We were seeing almost $6 for a dozen eggs, bread was $3.45 a loaf, even a bag of store brand pasta was $1.60. Chris did find his favorite treat though. 86 pesos for Dulce de Leche. Actually I just found the dulce de leche at 70 pesos. We heard from expats in the area that visiting the outdoor markets can be a cheaper place to get groceries. We found one in the Pocitos area where a dozen eggs was $4.60, which is a $1.40 cheaper than what we saw at the grocery store. The markets also sold plenty of fruits and vegetables, flowers, and even some household goods. On Sundays Rodo park has both a food market and an artisan market where you can buy all kinds of clothing and other goods. These are purses for yerba mate. One thing I noticed about Montevideo is how prominent the Yerba mate culture is here. Everywhere we went we saw people with their mates, with their straws, with their thermoses full of water. I loved seeing it so much and got so into it that I started asking people if I could take their photos with the yerba mate. You can buy your mate to hold your beverage in so many places in Montevideo and the prices can vary quite a bit. Though we saw a bunch in the $20 range. We heard from locals that the best mates or cups to buy are the ones made out of calabasa or squash. When we were looking at rental prices in Montevideo we found that apartment started at about $600 a month and could go up quite a bit from there depending on size, location, and amenities. Short-term rentals can be double or triple that and we found that three star hotels in the more touristy areas were starting at about $80 per night. For entertainment there is a lot you can do for free in the city. Ciudad Veijo or Oldtown has cool streets to walk down, plazas to check out, and street art that I really liked. Montevideo also has plenty of beautiful parks and places to be by the water. Now let's talk about visiting Colonia. Taking an in city bus on the COT bus lines between Montevideo and the cute little city of Colonia Del Sacramento costs about $14 for a one-way ticket. We noticed that the COT bus we were on seemed to have a lot more leg room than the Buquebus bus for less cost. Colonia is a former Portuguese settlement filled with cute cobblestone streets, places to buy souvenirs, and artist markets. I can't seem to stop myself from buying bracelets. Whenever I get to the market I just have to have one. This particular one the guy talked me into because I was looking at them and he told me that the uh the amethyst was the official stone of Uruguay. So by saying that he talked me into buying it. And now I have a new bracelet. You can go up to the lighthouse for about a dollar and explore the remains of the Old Town wall and the castle area for free. This is a draw bridge and under it would have been a moat. But the prices here seemed even higher than Montevideo. We saw bread here for $4 a loaf, and our one night very basic hotel room in Colonia was almost $100 for the night. I can see why travelers say that Uruguay is more expensive than other places they've visited in Latin America. For us coming from Buenos Aires we really had to adjust our price expectations and our spending, but Uruguay is definitely worth visiting. With its beautiful waterfront cities, cute little towns, and of course the Yerba mate culture. And that's our video for today we will see you in the next one.

recommendations: So we're going to check out the prices of goods and services, including food, rent, and transportation, in Uruguay's capital, Montevideo

videos.title: 7 things that frustrate me about living in Uruguay
videos.description:
I'm a Brit living in Montevideo, the capital of Uruguay 🇺🇾 I could be living anywhere in the world, but I chose Uruguay over two decades ago, and I absolutely love it 🫶

A reader asked what frustrates me about daily life here. So, here goes. These are some the things that irritate me—in no particular order—, about day to day life in Uruguay 🤯

#1: Convoluted tax system 💰

Uruguayans are sticklers for rules and regulations—and doing things by the book. To be honest, I appreciate it. It certainly flies in the face of stereotypes around Latin American corruption and bribery (don’t try to bribe a Uruguayan traffic cop, you could be arrested). And some bureaucracy in Uruguay like paying bills is now incredibly streamlined and easy. But not being able to do my own taxes as a sole trader, because the system is so convoluted, is definitely frustrating. I even know MBAs who threw up their hands and hired someone to do their taxes—which is what I quickly realised I needed to do too.

#2: Lack of glamour 👠

When asked what she wished she had known before she came to Uruguay, my friend Edie said she wished she had not wasted valuable luggage space on the expensive high heels she never got to wear. People don’t dress up much in Uruguay, not even when going out to dine or the theatre. While most of the time I love the freedom from convention, sometimes I confess, I long for a bit of glamour.

#3: Graffiti 🎨

The streets of Montevideo are lined with gorgeous art Deco buildings but unfortunately the walls are also riddled with graffiti and tags. There’s also outstanding street art but the tagging has made more than one unsuspecting visitor imagine that Montevideo had a serious gang problem. It really makes me sad and was bloody annoying when my own home was repeatedly graffitied.

#4: No real chips (french fries, for my US readers) 🍟🥔❌

Chips made from actual real potatoes sliced in a restaurant kitchen are hard to come by. In fact when I’ve asked at a restaurant if their chips were ‘real’, the proud response was “Yes, we only use [insert brandname of frozen fries]”. Fortunately the situation is improving.

#5: Poorly insulated homes 🏠❄️

Coming from a country where home insulation is a given, it can be a shock how poorly built and prepared houses are for the Uruguayan winter. Combined with high utility costs and damp from the ocean and the Rio de la Plata, many newcomers to Uruguay are condemned to a miserable winter when they arrive. My first winter in rented accommodation was truly miserable indoors—during the day it was actually colder inside the house than outside. Grrrr. Or rather, brrrr.

#6: Barking dogs 🐕🔊

Uruguayans absolutely love their dogs but in my experience they don’t tend to train them (well). To make it worse, owners tend to think that it’s perfectly normal for dogs to bark. A lot. It’s common to have several dogs for security purposes, especially in the countryside. Expect quizzical looks and limited action when you complain to your neighbor about their incessantly barking pooches.

#7: Poor customer service 👀

The customer is rarely right in Uruguay. I don't mind it that in general staff are not particularly friendly. But what is truly frustrating is to have a legitimate complaint, go to the cash register and be met by a defensive rather than a helpful or a sympathetic response.

Customer service has definitely started to improve. Businesses are aware of the situation and interestingly are choosing a lot of the recent arrivals that have come from Venezuela, Cuban, Dominicans, like super friendly really good idea of customer service. But neither expect automatic refunds, nor helpful service. It's so frustrating to have to accept a credit note even when the goods you're returning are faulty. But that is common practice here.

No place is perfect, right? Also see the seven things that I absolutely love about daily life in Uruguay.

Music: Nómade by Diego Janssen https://www.youtube.com/@diego_janssen

#uruguay #guruguay #thisisuruguay #livinginuruguay #montevideo #uruguaypros #uruguayprosandcons

videos.title: Uruguay 2025 Travel Guide! Are Montevideo and Colonia del Sacramento worth the trip?
videos.channel: Finding Gina Marie – Travel the World

Our Travel Gear: https://findingginamarie.com/gear
Briggs & Riley Collections: https://link.findingginamarie.com/brzdx
Ep.139 Uruguay is a quick ferry ride from Buenos Aires and doesn’t require a visa to visit. More importantly, because it’s one of the safest countries in South America, is a popular retirement spot due to its healthcare, and has a high quality of living, we decided to spend two weeks split between Colonia del Sacramento and Montevideo to check it out. As always, we break down all of our costs at the end.

traffic to get to the fery terminal can take a while depending on the time of year and the time of day the ferry takes an hour and 15 minutes and went by pretty quickly we didn't need to do anything with Uruguay immigration when we got to the other side we have a more in-depth article on our website about everything you'll want to know about Colonia Del Sacramento and Mont Vio and also all of the very sleepy town Christmas we grab a Christmas tree check out our website for all the places we ate in Colonia let's head to Mont Vio next we hopped on a cop bus heading east to Mont Vio the capital of Uruguay it took just under 3 hours to get here there's a bathroom on the bus if you need it which is a nice convenience so we're excited to be here in Monte pedo we took a bus from Colonia Dell Sacramento which was pretty easy and painless which is always a win the bus was on time so no problems at all really yeah I think the downside is this is a busier City but we're only going to be here for 6 days and we have a 3 a.m. departure when we finally do head out so that's going to be early but I think overall we're super excited to see one more City in uguay before we head out to Brazil a little bit of fast travel here now our favorite but we'll make the most of it after arriving at the bus terminal we took an Uber to our accommodations we weren't really sure what to think about the city as we were driving through it it seemed like a fairly nondescript place but the location of our Airbnb was great near Plaza constituion and Oldtown besides being the capital monteo is the largest city in Uruguay and to enjoy it at its best staying in the right location makes a huge difference on the weekend while we were there Plaza constitu had local Artisans and crafts people selling an assortment of items including what look like a small flea market this Plaza feels like the heart and soul of Oldtown we always enjoy beautiful architecture and Metropolitan Cathedral features neoc classical elements including a grand facade domes and twin bell towers as we headed towards the court at the other end of the Oldtown we enjoyed the Lively tunes of various Street musicians including this talented saxophonist if you've been to Colonia or Mont Vio or any other part of Uruguay we would love to hear your opinion about [Music] it yeah didn't nativo is in the buo neighborhood and is a small little Haven of native plants some street art and even small drums so that the visitors can interact with the garden and also connect with the afro Uruguayan gondom rhythms one of our favorite things is when a city has pedestrian only streets and Mont Vio has several vibrant street art is scattered all throughout Mont AO the combination of palm trees outdoor eating Street Music and colorful art combined to create a celebratory atmosphere we didn't grab an imperativo here but the area lends itself to chilling with one we enjoyed browsing the well preserved colonial era buildings with their ornate facades and Broad iron balconies reflecting the influence of Spanish and Portuguese Colonial Styles currency here is the euru and peso but we didn't need to get cash except when we stopped at EMP Carolina which has been located in the Marcato Del Puerto since 1960 they only take cash or debit cards so we incurred a small fee for using our debit card but it was worth it because they serve a huge variety of empanadas just make sure you have a backup choice because they can run out of some flavors and make sure you don't visit on a Sunday because they are closed on that day marato Del Puerto is mostly famous for steak restaurants and it's bustling during weekend afternoons you can smell the smoke from the fired Meats so this may not be the best place of your vegetarian depending on when you go the area can also be very Lively we visited the week before Christmas and we celebrated our 41st wedding anniversary here we were headed Elsewhere for Cappuccino but Cafe brazero Drew Us in it's the oldest Cafe in the city founded in 1877 and has attracted artists writers and musicians over its 140e history R CA prero in monteo and I've got some Cafe collee a little mini with ham and cheese chos Del they look they look very Dolce with this one I'm I'm going to go with less sugar on the first one how's that is it looks too much sweet for me a little is this this look almost looks like caramel look like chocolate that's what it is it's not chocolate I actually like these better than the ones we had in Madrid these are juic here and Sugar's not bad either pretty tasty our Airbnb was inside a building with 24-hour security so he always felt safe it also had a food market on the bottom floor which made it very convenient to grab ingredients for a quick meal especially since a lot of establishments were closed on Sundays we didn't take video of our meal at lbla because we had planned to return and we were so bummed that we never were able to get back there Kevin had chivito which is a sandwich with Chasco beef bacon a fried egg ham green olives mozzarella tomatoes and mayonnaise it's the national dish of Uruguay with good reason and it broke my heart that we didn't get to go back we celebrated our 41st wedding anniversary with a dinner at El fogan restaurant which is a steakhouse founded in 1962 so we're at El fogan for our anniversary dinner or one years's Captain Mary and I've got the MRE which is a beautiful kind of steak and some provincial mushrooms with it which I can smell the garlic already smells good it's not as tender as I would expect doesn't matter it's still a great flavor well the mushrooms are perfectly very buttery yeah delicious very nice place very authentic feels like it's been around here forever and really nice dinner we didn't need a reservation but you should have one there are a lot of locals that frequent here we decided to take a walk along L rambla for a bit but it spans more than 22 km along the Rio de La Plata continuing down the entire coast of Mont Vio it's not the most beautiful promov we've been on but it's a nice walk otherwise named for the old gas company cadel gas is a small rocky Beach where plenty of locals were fishing but it's Rocky and really not a nice place for laying out on the sand it's also quite smelly although you can see that there are some people who did lay out towering over Plaza independencia the main square of Mont Vio is palasio Salvo this Italian Gothic and Art Deco building was designed to be a 27 story luxury hotel in 1928 but it ended up becoming a mix of private residences and offices it houses a music studio a radio station the Uruguayan Tango Museum and a cinema at the the southwest corner of Plaza independencia is the Magnificent tiatro Solis which opened its doors to this Grand Theater for the first time in 1856 this neoclassical designed building has hosted performances of Opera ballet theater and concerts the significant portions of Mont vdo's fortification walls that were built in the late 18th century have been dismantled due to the city's expansion and modernization but the beautiful architecture of the Gateway of the Citadel remains so what do we think about our time in monteo I think we liked it well enough and we really enjoyed exploring the Old Town it was an easy laid-back place to visit with a vibrant cultural scene the influence of African Uruguayan culture especially through kandom and Tango was something I'm interested in learning more about we wouldn't call this a bucket list destination but if you're visiting Uruguay we think mono is worth a few days of your time so what did we spend for 13 days in Uruguay let's start with Colonia Del Sacramento dining cost us $39 and groceries were $43 which breaks down to be $25.3 per person per day our accommodations were $467 which breaks down to $66.75 per night our bouquet bus Ferry tickets were just under $69 per person excluding the cost to get to Colonia Del Sacramento we spent a total of $819 here which breaks down to be $585 per person per day in Mont Vio we ate at an Empanada restaurant that only took debit cards or cash so our bank charged us a small 23 C fee for using our debit card our dining was cheaper in Mont Vio at $187 but our groceries were double the price of Colonia Del Sacramento at $855 which breaks down to be $22.76 per person per day I got my hair colored here for $87.54 which includes a tip we used Ubers to get two and from our accommodations for $19.68 lodging was only $335 and5 and we liked them much better here too the cost breaks down to be $558 per day now technically we booked for 7 days because we got a discount for staying a week if we had only booked for 6 days instead of seven it actually would have cost us $23 more the bus from Colonia Del Sacramento cost us $45.10 or $225 a piece excluding the cost to get to Mont Vio we spent a total of $755 excluding the hair salon from Mont Vio you can see that it's actually a cheaper city than in Colonia Del Sacramento we spent $241 per person less per day for food in Mont Vio and lodging was $10.92 less per day we spent $1,534 128 for 13 days in Uruguay EX including the travel cost to both cities that breaks down to be $118.2 per day or $591 per person per day that's all we have from Mont Vio we're on our way to a 3:00 a.m. flight to Rio De videos.title: TOP 10 Things to do in Montevideo, Uruguay 2023!
videos.description:
TOP 10 Things to do in Montevideo, Uruguay 2023! 

Montevideo, Uruguay is an amazing place to visit in Uruguay. If you want to know top 10 what to do in Montevideo or you need a travel guide, please keep watching.

Moving on to number 5, we recommend Taking a tour of Teatro Solis: This historic theater showcases breathtaking architecture and rich heritage. By taking a tour, you'll delve into its captivating history, learn about its role in Uruguay's cultural scene, and appreciate its grandeur. Immerse yourself in the world of performing arts and experience the magic that has unfolded on its stage for centuries.

At number 4, we recommend Experiencing Montevideo's nightlife in Pocitos and Punta Carretas: These neighborhoods offer an array of bars, clubs, and restaurants, where you can dance to Latin rhythms, indulge in delicious cuisine, and mingle with locals and fellow travelers alike. The energetic ambiance and diverse nightlife scene make it an unforgettable experience in Montevideo.

At number 3 - Visiting Museo del Carnaval: The Museo del Carnaval in Montevideo is a must-visit attraction for anyone interested in immersing themselves in Uruguay's rich carnival culture. This museum offers a captivating journey through the history, traditions, and vibrant costumes associated with the country's famous carnival celebrations. Through its interactive exhibits and displays, visitors can gain a deeper understanding and appreciation for this integral part of Uruguay's cultural heritage.

Now, let's unveil number 2 on our list— Enjoy a mate by the beach: Sipping this traditional South American herbal tea while overlooking the beautiful coastline creates a sense of relaxation and connection with nature. It's a chance to embrace Uruguay's social ritual and enjoy the tranquility of the beach simultaneously.

Finally, securing the number 1 spot on our list is Taking a day trip to Colonia del Sacramento: This UNESCO World Heritage Site offers cobblestone streets, colonial architecture, and a peaceful atmosphere. Explore the old Portuguese fortress, admire the picturesque riverfront, and immerse yourself in the town's unique blend of European and South American influences. It's a delightful escape from the bustling city life of Montevideo.
